content.plain_english: This week in Year 4 maths, your child is working on Shape. The learning focuses on: identify lines of symmetry in 2d shapes presented in different orientations and complete a simple symmetric figure with respect to a specific line of symmetry. These skills build directly on what they have already mastered and prepare them for the next steps in the programme of study.
content.in_school: Children handle, sort, and classify 2-D and 3-D shapes, drawing and describing their properties. They use protractors, mirrors, and squared paper to explore angles, symmetry, and position. Explaining what they notice in precise mathematical language is a key part of every lesson.
content.home_activity: Go on a shape hunt around the house. How many rectangles can you spot? Any cylinders in the kitchen? Count vertices and edges on a cereal box or tin. For angles, look for right angles (corners of doors) and ask whether other angles are acute or obtuse.
dinner_table_questions: Can you show me how to identify lines of symmetry in 2d shapes presented in different orientations?
Was it easy or tricky to complete a simple symmetric figure with respect to a specific line of symmetry? Why?
How would you explain today's maths to someone who has never seen it before?

key_vocabulary.definition: A flat shape with length and width but no thickness — e.g. triangle, circle.
A solid shape with length, width, and height — e.g. cube, sphere, cone.
When one half of a shape is a mirror image of the other.
An angle of exactly 90° — the same as the corner of a square.
Lines that meet at a right angle (90°).

example_questions: Plot the point (3, 5) on a coordinate grid.
Translate the shape 2 right and 3 up.
Name a quadrilateral with exactly one pair of parallel sides.
content.times_table_tip: Year 4 children should know all their times tables to 12 × 12 by the end of the year — the national Multiplication Tables Check happens in Year 4. Daily 3-minute practice mixing tables in random order is the best preparation.
